inline_comments.message: why is this added as xpath value in fragment table?
why is this added as xpath value in fragment table?
I think would be better named as ComparativeOperatorType, Angular is not commonly used for <> from what I can see, opinions from team needed

Would Avoid the acronyms,something like:
GREATER
LESS
GREATER_OR_EQUAL
LESS_OR_EQUAL

Is it ok if you use:
angularOperatorsQueue == null || angularOperatorsQueue.isEmpty()
For some reason the CollectionUtils package is breaking an Ericsson pipeline sorry :)
Same with other comment on CollectionUtils
Would change to 20 as current test result only proves = part of >=

Please add some tests using AND/OR e.g. //books[@price>10 or @lang="English"]
From what I see, boolean conditions don't work with comparison operator.
For current changes Boolean condition is not working with comparison operators, Need to find/write proper query which supports both conditions.

Can you change this to just check book title? It will make test easier to read.
        then: 'the result contains expected nodes'
            def bookTitles = result.collect { it.getLeaves().get('title') }
            assert bookTitles.sort() == expectedBookTitles.sort()

and for the test cases:
 scenario            | cpspath               || expectedBookTitles
 'the ">" condition' | '//books[@price>13]'  || ["The Gruffalo", "Annihilation", "The Light Fantastic", 
                                                 "A Book with No Language", "Debian GNU/Linux"]
Okay, as long as it is a documented limitation.

It would also be nice to see a test case that is expected to return nothing, e.g.

 scenario            | cpspath                || expectedBookTitles
 'price > 1000'      | '//books[@price>1000]' || []

It would also be interesting to see if negative numbers are supported:
 'negative numbers'  | '//books[@price>-100]' || ["The Gruffalo", "Annihilation", ...]

(this case could be done here or in CpsPathQuerySpec)

The problem is e.g. //books[@price>10 or @lang="English"] if we want to query this
Native Query : ((attributes  ->>'price')\:\:int > '10' and attributes @> '{"lang":"English"}') ; were postgress is not supporting this operation . So for now it will be as limitation.
Good refactoring, I think this will make it easier to support AND/OR later.
NB: You may enrich unit testing having comprehensive descendant path like //child[@code=2]/books[@price>260]

NB: You may enrich unit tests having descendant cps path like this:

'the "<" condition  where there is a result'     | '//categories[@code=3]/books[@price<13]' || 1                 | ['The Colour of Magic']
why dont you add EQ to this too ?!

refactor this using the spearet rules I suggested in the g4 file.
So you have 2 separate exit methods 
kepe any common code in a private method
this should become less complex when you use separate condition for string and integer...
if refactored correctly I dont think you need this check anymore
use teh same names as in th G4 file (GT, LT, GE, LE)
this is gettignt o complex, please extract out method, possibly several methods to enhance readability

you are not using combinations?!
I wonder with my suggestions at teh parser wil it work with combinatiosn too...

above are just some example not exactly the one your are using, it does raise the question/opportunity to support both => and >= 
and <= and =<)
No problem, if needed we will take for other commit
if EQ is added need to change all other rules where EQ is there so i didnt do, But now i have include EQ in comparative rule
Earlier I have used EG,GE,GT,LT later got comments to use fullform for clear understanding so t again added fullform. Now resolved this as per antlr doc.
Since ,Used single for both integer and string refactored this method accordingly.

Depending upon leafvalue we have 2 different queries 
 Eg :  //books[@lang="English" and price=15]
       ( attributes  @> '{"lang":"English"}) for string
       (attributes  ->>'price')\:\:int = '15') for integer
 In order to add both together used queryleafconditionmethod

Revert this change please, it should use EQ, not comparativeOperators:
  textFunctionCondition : SLASH leafName OB KW_TEXT_FUNCTION EQ StringLiteral CB ;

Otherwise we would allow things like "abcd"<123 - string/number comparison not allowed!
The other below changes are good though.
NB: you could change his to comparativeOperators  too, it wil help catching specific errors

I don't think you can reach this code!  because it wouldn't match the leafCondition rule
Unless you have a test (i would be very curious to see  it) that actually covers this you can remove this line
please show me any test, i dont think these null-check are needed and they will drive down code coverage stats
please show me any test, i dont think these null-check are needed and they will drive down code coverage stats
again, is it possible it is NOT set?! I dont think so

NB: capital P is actual correct although we might not have been consistent in the past
so i you want you can set all occurrences in this file to cpsPath
AT leafName EQ StringLiteral | AT leafName comparativeOperators IntegerLiteral
What are these operators used for? When I tested, I got SQL ERROR: "operator does not exist: integer =< unknown"

oops, I had assumed SQL would support them too. If not we have to remove them again
Its inconsistent with xpath language but we can live with it and I don't think anyone will notice
(as long as we clearly document the supported operators in the forthcomming dcx)
I though i have written test scenario for this but i didnt write any test.yes, this is throwing error

Hmm, this change made the CpsPathBuilder a lot more complex.

Also, I forgot that we do allow mixing integer/string literals, for example both of these are valid in our current system:
- @price=10
- @price='10'

This is a little tricky, because we don't want to allow any comparison with strings, like @title < "abc". But we do want to store "=" as comparativeOperator, even for string. I can't think of an easy solution, because the parser doesn't know the actual types of the leaves (that info is only in the yang model). I guess it is okay if we let the SQL query handle those cases.

So we should just revert this change, to keep the implementation simple.

So, now parser rule will be
leafCondition : AT leafName comparativeOperators StringLiteral | AT leafName comparativeOperators IntegerLiteral ?
we can also just us a bit of java if we combine a StringLiteral with anything but = to throw an exception (sometimes a bit of java can prevent complexity in the G4 file)

Yeah, rule can be:
  leafCondition : AT leafName comparativeOperators StringLiteral | AT leafName comparativeOperators IntegerLiteral ;
don't understand who this can be null..

OK, I see now it can be null for an incomplete expression
(I never understand why Antlr parser allows incomplete expressions... but that is for a future improvement)
For now I have done up a neater and more performant solution in the fromString() method, I'll send you a patch
yes for missing attribute value . OK

make sure to ALWAYS poll (before the if else) otherwise the operators can get mixed up!
e.g.  consider and write a test for @Lang='German' and @price>10

check that the operator is '=' if NOT throw an CPSPathException
(add a test for this scenario too)
you can extend this test (use a where block) to also cover the new scenario with @Lang>'German'...
add a where with 2 scnerios to this test instead of below, more explaantions in comments below

'cpsPath that cannot be parsed' | ''cpsPath that cannot be parsed''
